coredata.dc:title: An electrocatalytic active AuNPs/5-Amino-2-mercaptobenzimidazole/rGO/SPCE composite electrode for ultrasensitive detection of progesterone
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaoyu
Zheng, Lixiao
Yan, Yujia
Cao, Ruge
Zhang, Juankun
coredata.dc:description: Progesterone is a precursor of many steroidal hormones and associated with the stabilization and control of hormonal levels in the reproductive cycle. Herein, the use of reduced graphene oxide (rGO), 5-Amino-2-mercaptobenzimidazole (AMBI) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) on Screen-Printing carbon electrode (SPCE) electrode as a modified electrode of electrochemical sensors for voltammetric determination of progesterone (P4) is described for the first time. Characterization was performed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectra. The properties of the modified electrode were examined by cyclic voltammetry (CV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Under operating voltage of −1.64 V, the determination is performed by square wave voltammetry. Figures of merits such as linear dynamic response for P4 concentration ranging from 0.9 × 10−9 to 27 × 10−6 mol L−1, limit of detection of 0.28 × 10−9 mol L−1. For the first time, AuNP/AMBI/rGO electrode has been constructed and applied for high-performance P4 sensing, with potential for development in further electrochemical sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reduced graphene oxide 5-Amino-2-mercaptobenzimidazole gold nanoparticles catalytic reduction electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical behavior for direct reduction of P4
Reproducibility, stability, selectivity and sensitivity of prepared sensors
Analysis in calf serum and milk

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The procedure for fabrication of AuNP/AMBI/rGO platform.
SEM images of: (a) GO, (b) AMBI/GO, and (c) AuNP/AMBI/rGO surfaces. TEM images: (d) AuNP/AMBI/rGO surfaces.

(a) XRD pattern of: GO, AMBI/GO and AuNP/AMBI/rGO. (b) Raman spectra of GO, AMBI/GO and AuNP/AMBI/rGO. XPS spectra of AuNP/AMBI/rGO nanocomposite: (c) C1s line, (d) N1s line, (e) S2p line, and (f) Au4f line.
CV curves (A) and EIS curves (B) of different electrodes in 0.2 M phosphate buffer (pH = 6) consisting of 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] and 0.1 M KCl: (a) bare SPCE, (b) AMBI/GO/SPCE, (c) AuNP/AMBI/rGO/SPCE.

(a) The relationship between the current density and potential of P4 (15 mol·L−1) obtained by different electrodes. Inset is schematic diagram of reduction of P4 process on surface of SPCE(left) and AuNP/AMBI/rGO/SPCE(right) (b) Square wave voltammograms obtained for AuNP/AMBI/rGO/SPCE for successive additions of P4 in range of 0.0009–27.0 μM. (c) Linear relationship between cathodic peak current and P4 concentration. Inset is a partial enlargement of linear relationship.
AuNP/AMBI/rGO/SPCE electrode current and current fluctuation amplitude in relation to storage time in aqueous solution consisting of 0.1 M NaOH and 15 μmol L−1 P4.

Histogram of the changes of sensor response to P4 in the absence and the presence of interferences in milk (a) and calf serum (b). (P4 concentration: 15 μmol·L−1 and interferences concentration: 1500 μmol·L−1). Abscissa: (1) P4, (2) P4 + L-Cystine, (3) P4 + Glycine, (4) P4 + L-Cysteine, (5) P4 + L-Arginine, (6) P4 + Glucose, (7) P4 + l-Ascorbic acid, (8) P4 + Folic acid, (9) P4 + Riboflavin, (10) P4 + Nicotinic acid, (11) P4 + Tocopherol, (12) P4 + Vitamin B6, (13) P4 + Vitamin A, (14) P4 + Estradiol, (15) P4 + Estriol, (16) P4 + Urea.

head.ce:title: An electrocatalytic active AuNPs/5-Amino-2-mercaptobenzimidazole/rGO/SPCE composite electrode for ultrasensitive detection of progesterone

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, College of Food Engineering and Biotechnology, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, College of Food Engineering and Biotechnology
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, College of Food Engineering and Biotechnology, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Progesterone is a precursor of many steroidal hormones and associated with the stabilization and control of hormonal levels in the reproductive cycle. Herein, the use of reduced graphene oxide (rGO), 5-Amino-2-mercaptobenzimidazole (AMBI) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) on Screen-Printing carbon electrode (SPCE) electrode as a modified electrode of electrochemical sensors for voltammetric determination of progesterone (P4) is described for the first time. Characterization was performed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectra. The properties of the modified electrode were examined by cyclic voltammetry (CV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Under operating voltage of −1.64 V, the determination is performed by square wave voltammetry. Figures of merits such as linear dynamic response for P4 concentration ranging from 0.9 × 10−9 to 27 × 10−6 mol L−1, limit of detection of 0.28 × 10−9 mol L−1. For the first time, AuNP/AMBI/rGO electrode has been constructed and applied for high-performance P4 sensing, with potential for development in further electrochemical sensing applications.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Reduced graphene oxide 5-Amino-2-mercaptobenzimidazole gold nanoparticles catalytic reduction electrochemical sensor

ce:section.ce:para: The sensitive detection of P4 was achieved by using specific binding of antigen-antibody presently [13]. Several electrochemical biosensors have been reported for the determination of P4 [14,15]. P4 determination based on enzymatic immunoassay in milk have been developed using anti-progesterone antibody adsorbed on the surface of carbon electrodes [16,17]. However, the antibody is difficult to immobilized and maintain long-term activity. Moreover, the transport and storage of antibodies cost much since their high sensitivity to the environment. Therefore, many researchers have been dedicated to studies involving modification of electrode surfaces in order to achieve a simpler and more sensitive method for detecting progesterone [18]. Electrochemical biosensors involving the use of nanomaterials with catalytic properties to enhance signal have demonstrated to possess interesting features. We proposed a solution by combining the AuNPs modified AMBI on SPCE/rGO for catalytically reduce the progesterone to form a dimer. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been applied as emerging material for wide variety of biomedical, chemical sensing, and photocatalysis applications in recent years due to its practical advantages such as easy preparation, large surface area, high electrode conductivity, excellent catalysis and irreplaceable biocompatibility [19]. In particular, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been often used to amplify the current intensity and improve the accuracy of analysis of electrochemical sensors. The biologically active 5-Amino-2-mercaptobenzimidazole (AMBI), a component of vitamin B12 with similar structure to the purine bases of DNA, has obvious catalytic effect on the reaction of active substances in vivo [20]. More importantly, surface assembly of gold–sulfur binding not only improve the catalytic activity of sulfur-containing catalysts by virtue of the redox reaction, but also further modify the physical and chemical properties of AuNPs [21]. As a nanoscale carbonaceous material, graphene and its derivatives were often considered as preferred support and modification material for catalysts with advantages of easy manufacture, low-cost inputs, and high sensitivity to biomolecules [22]. In addition, hydrophilicity and thermal stability of GO can effectively tune the homogeneous dispersion of other particles on its own surface, activity, selectivity, and stability of the catalyst, and further facilitate the assembly of macroscopic structures by active binding, which in turn boost the overall performance of sensor. It has been well documented that screen-printing carbon electrode (SPCE) plays an essential role in designing of disposable biosensors and electrochemical sensors because of its surface features, simple manufacturing process, low material cost, green and non-toxic finished products, which are quite appropriate for mass manufacturing [23,24].

Here, we prepared a composite electrode of electrochemical sensor for P4 determination by implementing catalytic reduction of SPCE/rGO substrate by AuNPs modified AMBI. Under alkaline conditions, the peak current of P4 was measured by square wave voltammetry to convert the relative concentration. This composite electrode exhibits accurate selectivity, low detection limit with simple process for the application of P4 determination.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized by a modified Hummers method [25]. 5-Amino-2-mercaptobenzimidazole (AMBI), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), progesterone (P4)，N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimidehydroc hloride (EDCI), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium chloride (KCl) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (www.sigmaaldrich.com) and employed without any further purification. Double-distilled water was prepared in our laboratory system. P4 was dissolved in the ethanol stock solution (1.0 mmol·L−1), and then diluted in NaOH (0.1 mol·L−1) solution with appropriate amount.

All electrochemical experiments were carried out on the electrochemical workstation (IVIUM Compact Stat. e20250). In the electrode system, the working electrode, the reference electrode and the counter electrode were bare or modified SPCE, Ag/AgCl and platinum, respectively. SPCE is a self-made disposable electrode. The conductive carbon slurry was add to the acetone and cyclohexanone (V:V = 1:1) at a ratio of 2:1, and mixed well. 2 g of diluted conductive carbon slurry was coated on the PET board with a spatula, with a thickness of 0.05 mm. At last, it was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 20 min. Morphology characterization was performed by SEM (Hitachi SU8020, Japan) and TEM (JEOL 2100 HRTEM, Japan). The structural characterization was performed by XRD (XD-3, China), Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw Invia plus, Britain), and XPS (Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi, USA).

Electrochemical techniques were used to characterize the bare or modified electrodes using cyclic voltammetry (CV), impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and square wave voltammetry (SWV). The CV was tested in potential range of −0.2 to 0.6 V with scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The EIS was tested in frequency range of 0.1 to 100 kHz with amplitude of 5 mV. The above experiments were tested in 0.2 M phosphate buffer (pH = 6) containing 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] and 0.1 M KCl. Under optimized conditions, SWV was used to determine P4 in 0.1 M sodium hydroxide. The determination conditions were tested at a scanning potential of −1.8 ~ −1.4 V, a frequency of 5 Hz, a square wave amplitude of 40 mV, and a potential increase of 4 mV.

First, the synthesized GO was dispersed in ethanol and ultrasound for 2 h to make a 2 mg mL−1 suspension, and then the GO suspension was ice bathed with 0.2 M EDCI and NHS solution for 6 h to activate the carboxyl group of GO. The resulting activated GO suspension was mixed with AMBI (1.0 mM) in a ratio of 1:1 (V/V) at room temperature for 5 h. GO/AMBI was obtained by centrifugation and dispersed with ultrasound in ultrapure water for further use.

The preparation of AuNPs was firstly to dissolve 0.01 M chloroauric acid in 100 mL of boiling water, rapidly added 0.025 M sodium citrate and stirred under heating for 15 min to obtain a nano gold sol. Thereafter, AMBI/GO (30 mg mL−1) was added in a ratio of 1:1 (V/V), and a mixture of 0.3 M sodium hydroxide and 0.45 M sodium borohydride was added dropwise. GO was reduced to rGO under continuously stirring. AuNP/AMBI/rGO was obtained by centrifugation and dispersed with ultrasound in ultrapure water for further use.

The chemical compositions and structures of GO, AMBI/GO, AuNP/AMBI/rGO were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy. The elemental composition of AuNP/AMBI/rGO was studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The morphologies of each electrodes were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The preparation of the AuNP/AMBI/rGO composite electrode was schematically shown in Fig. 1 .

The standard addition method was used to determine the content of P4 in calf serum and milk. In brief, 1 mL of fresh calf serum was diluted to 10 mL with 0.1 M NaOH solution, and a defined concentration of P4 was added to a known volume of diluted calf serum without further treatment. The resulting solution was transferred to an electrochemical cell for subsequent analysis of P4. Milk sample was prepared in accordance with calf serum.
Parameters including the volume of the prepared suspension, the pH of the buffer, the enrichment time and square wave frequency were optimized and discussed in Fig. S1–4.

Typically, the prepared material was dispersed in deionized water under ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. A uniformly dispersed suspension with concentration of 2 mg mL−1 was formed. 10 uL of suspension was added to the SPCE electrode dropwise, followed by drying at 40 °C for 10 min and storing in vacuum for further use.

It has been documented that graphene contains a large number of acidic carboxyl groups that can interact with active basic amino groups [26–29]. So, 1-(3-aminopropyl) imidazole (API) and 2-aminoethanethiol (2-AET) successfully recombined with graphene [26,30]. By the same token, AMBI can react with acidic carboxyl on GO to form a ππ conjugated bond between the benzimidazole and graphene. And this combination is very stable. It has been proved by the literature that it can exist stably between pH 5–13 without being dissociated [26,30]. Reduced GO has better conductivity and is a good substrate for composite materials [30]. Based on above conclusion, the sensing ability are expected to be improved by following mechanism: The unshared electron pair of the nitrogen atom at the 1 position of the benzimidazole group in AMBI participates in cyclic conjugation, which makes AMBI easy to lose electrons and catalyze P4 to form a dimer. The transition instability time is very short, and AMBI will quickly obtain electrons to form a stable structure again, so the catalytic performance can always be kept efficient. After AuNPs is bound to the sulfhydryl group on AMBI, it can increase the catalytic activity of AMBI and increase the conductivity [31,32]. The synergistic effect of three materials was expected to the benefit of better determination of P4.

The morphology of the synthesized materials was preliminarily studied by SEM. As displayed in Fig. 2a, it shows the flake composition with smooth surface, flat edges and obvious stacking of lamellar structures. The sheet structure in Fig. 2b is still visible. The carboxyl groups are mostly distributed on the edge of the graphene oxide sheet. By adding N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimidehydroc hloride (EDCI) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) to GO in ice bath, these carboxyl groups were activated. Then the addition of AMBI caused the flake graphene sheets to wrinkle and the distance between the lamellar layers increased. The addition of AMBI caused the flake graphene sheets to wrinkle and the distance between the lamellar layers increased. It could be clearly seen that GO was no longer stacked together in an orderly manner with the edges and surface become quite rough. Fig. 2c shows that the particle coverage of the AuNPs is well distributed on the surface of the graphene. Graphene's thin layers becomes more wrinkled and disordered, this wrinkle property was beneficial for maintaining a high surface area on the electrode because the flakes was not easily folded back to the graphite structure.

The results of AuNP/AMBI/rGO composite in TEM are shown in Fig. 2d. The lighter shaded substrate at the bottom was a thin layer of graphene, that the smooth edges clearly visible. The circular uniform-sized particles were nano‑gold particles, which were well dispersed on the surface of the graphene without agglomeration, indicating that it has been bound to the graphene.

The XRD spectra of GO, AMBI/GO and AuNP/AMBI/rGO are shown in Fig. 3a. The XRD pattern of GO, AMBI/GO had a dominant peak at 2θ = 10.5, which was a characteristic peak unique to GO. The peak at 2θ = 43 in the XRD pattern is a characteristic peak of the 100 crystal plane of graphene oxide [33]. This peak disappeared in the AuNP/AMBI/rGO diffraction pattern, indicating that GO was almost completely reduced to rGO. This reduction process represented the reconstruction of the conjugated graphene network sp2 in the epoxidation ring-opening reaction. The peak at 2θ = 20 is a characteristic peak of graphite. When graphene oxide reacts with AMBI, part of the graphene oxide will fold back into the graphite structure under the interaction of van der Waals force and ππ stacking. The AuNPs dispersed on the rGO exhibit new diffraction peaks at 2θ = 38.1, 44.2, 64.4, and 77.5, corresponding to the crystal planes of (111,200,220), and (311) of the gold structures. No diffraction peaks from any other impurities were detected.

The Raman spectra of GO, AMBI/GO and AuNP/AMBI/rGO are shown in Fig. 3b. The carbon materials containing GO and AMBI/GO had characteristic signals on the G-band at 1590 cm−1 and the D-band at 1347 cm−1. In addition, the positions of G and D peaks of AuNP/AMBI/rGO were slightly different from GO and AMBI/GO due to the doping effect induced by AuNPs. A moderate red shift of 5 cm−1 could be detected from 1590 cm−1 (for GO) to 1595 cm−1 (for AuNP/AMBI/rGO). This shift indicated a strong interaction between AuNPs and GO.

In the XPS spectra, the peaks of C1s (Fig. 3c), N1s (Fig. 3d), S2p (Fig. 3e) and Au4f (Fig. 3f) demonstrated the successful synthesis of AuNP/ AMBI /rGO nanocomposite. The peaks on 284.8 eV, 286.9 eV and 288.7 eV were characteristic peaks for CC, CO and -CONH of the C1s spectrum respectively. The peak of the N1s spectrum at 399.8 eV was a characteristic peak of the NH group of the amide. The peak of 402.0 eV was attributed to the unreacted NH groups on the heterocycle of AMBI. The fitting curve at S2p region was split into two 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 energy levels. The S2p signal could be deconvoluted into an S2p 3/2 and S2p 1/2 doublet at 161.1, 163.7 and 164.9 eV, respectively, with the intensity ratio of 1:2, indicating the reaction with AuNPs. The Au4f 7/2 peak at 84.1 eV demonstrates the successful binding of sulfhydryl-Au for AMBI, while the 87.8 eV signal could also be assigned to AuNPs that did not undergo an electrical reaction. The atomic percentages of C, N, S, and Au on the sample surface were 89.36%, 4.09%, 0.37%, and 6.17%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior for direct reduction of P4
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4A compares the activity of bare SPCE and two modified SPCE in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- (0.1 M KCl) electrolyte. The bare SPCE (curve a) showed an oxidation peak at 131.87 uA. In contrast, the anode peak potential of the SPCE modified by GO/AMBI (curve b) was 150.73 uA, and the peak current increase of 14.30% relative to bare SPCE. For final composite form of AuNP/AMBI/rGO, the anode peak current increased by 46.28% to 192.91 uA (curve c). The signal and peak enhancement of the final synthesized material indicated that the redox activity of the AuNP/AMBI/rGO modified SPCE electrode was significantly increased, due to the higher electron transfer rate. EIS was an effective method for probing the features of electrode surface. The corresponding Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. 4B. The obtained EIS data was fitted using a simple Randles equivalent circuit consisting of the electrolyte solution resistance (Rs), the electron-transfer resistance (Rct), constant phase element (CDL), and Warburg impedance element (ZW). As a results, the Rct value of SPCE (curve a), AMBI/GO/SPCE (curve b) and AuNP/AMBI/rGO/SPCE (curve c) were 178.9 Ω, 66.4 Ω, 47.9 Ω respectively, indicating that the modification of AuNP/AMBI/rGO improved the rate of electron transfer and the electrode active area.

The effective surface areas of SPCE, GO/SPCE, AMBI/GO/SPCE, AuNP/AMBI/rGO/SPCE were 3.235, 3.556, 4.605, and 5.202 cm−2 calculated by using the Randle-Sevcik equation. Fig. 5a shows the relationship between current density and potential when P4 is reduced by different materials: SPCE, GO/SPCE, AMBI/GO/SPCE and AuNP/AMBI/rGO/SPCE. Compared with unmodified screen printed electrodes, the current density of AuNP/AMBI/rGO/SPCE electrodes increased by 2.4 times, proving that better responses can be obtained with surface modification of AuNP/AMBI/rGO. Furthermore, for AuNP/AMBI/rGO/SPCE, P4 exhibited a peak current density higher significantly in contrast to GO/SPCE or AMBI/GO/SPCE, indicating that the synergistic contribution of AuNPs, AMBI and GO could remarkably increase the reduction efficiency of P4.The cathodic process observed at 1.64 V was related to the reduction of the hormone on electrode surface. It has been proved that the benzimidazole group could act as a cocatalyst in this system, participate in the redox process, or play a role in promoting the formation of the electroactive species, or act as a nucleophile on the carbonyl group, facilitating the cleavage of the bond. A possible mechanism is proposed and illustrated in inset of Fig. 5a. The unshared electron pair of the 1-position nitrogen atom of the five-membered aromatic heterocyclic ring in the AMBI participated in the cyclic conjugate, and the electron density of the nitrogen atom decreased, making the hydrogen on the nitrogen atom easy to remove in the form of hydrogen ion. Under certain conditions, AMBI lost electrons and catalyzed the P4 to form a dimer. The transition state of AMBI after losing electrons was particularly unstable, so it could quickly obtain electrons to form a stable structure again. As a result, the catalytic performance remained highly efficient. Based on the advantages of AuNPs itself, its modification improved the catalytic performance of AMBI and increased the overall electron flow.

In alkaline medium, the dimerization occurred originally by a single electron reaction with the reduction of the C-3 keto group of the P4 molecule and the formation of an unprotonated radical. The carbonyl carbon atom of the C-3 ketone group of P4 molecule possessed a higher positive electron density than than the C-20 ketone group, and it was easier to obtain electrons to become radical anions. After the radical anion undergoed protonation, it reacted with another unprotonated radical to form a dimer.

The following parameters were optimized: (a) volume of the prepared suspension; (b) the pH of the buffer; (c) the enrichment time; (d) square wave frequency. Respective text and Figures on optimizations are given in the Electronic Supporting Material. In short, the optimization results were given as: (a) optimal volume: 10 μL; (b) optimal pH: 13; (c) optimal time: 0 s. (d) square wave frequency: 5 Hz.

The square wave voltammetry under optimal conditions (Fig. 5b) shows that the peak current increases linearly with the increase of P4 concentration. The dashed line in Fig. 5b is the SWV result for pure sodium hydroxide solution without the addition of P4. Each point of the calibration graph was corresponded to the mean value obtained from five independent measurements. The spectrum exhibited a linear behavior in the concentration range of 0.0009–27 μmol·L−1 (Fig. 5c). The limit of quantification (LOQ, ten times of standard deviation the blank signal/slope) and the limit of detection (LOD, three times of standard deviation the blank signal/slope) were calculated to be 0.93 and 0.28 nmol·L−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, stability, selectivity and sensitivity of prepared sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Three AuNP/AMBI/rGO modified electrodes fabricated and tested independently under the same conditions The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the three signals was 1.29% indicating the excellent reproducibility of the sensor.

It was prepared under optimal conditions and stored in a desiccator in order to evaluate the long-term stability of the prepared AuNP/AMBI/rGO modified electrode. The electrochemical response of P4 at a concentration of 15 μmol·L−1 was measured under the same background solution conditions. The test was performed every 10 days, and three samples were tested independently. As shown in Fig. 6 , the current response decreases by about 6% compared with the initial current after 50 days of measurement and the RSD of the three parallel signals is between 1.35% and 2.68%, which indicates that the electrode had preeminent long-term stability. To sum up, the service life of the composite electrode is 50 days.

There are a large number of amino acids, sugars, vitamins, hormones and metabolites in milk and calf serum, and the main representative is selected as an interferent for interference experiments. 15 μM P4 and 1500 μM other interferences were added to the calf serum sample. The influence of 100 times the level of interference on the current signal of P4 is between −2.6% - +4.6% in the milk sample (Fig. 7a). The effect of 100 times the level of interference on the current signal of P4 is −3.2% ± 3.2% in the calf serum sample (Fig. 7b). l-ascorbic acid and riboflavin both have a large effect on the current of P4 in both systems, but the increase or decrease of the current is within the error range, which does not affect the determination performance of the sensor, indicating its good selectivity. There were no other substances that can be detected at the same voltage.

At a concentration of P4 of 15 μmol·L−1, the electrochemical responses of three samples were independently tested to study their sensitivity (Table 1 ). The sensor sensitivity was obtained by current value divided by analyte concentration and electrode area. The calculated sensitivity is 1.132 μA·μM−1·cm−2, and the RSD is 1.5%, which indicates its good sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis in calf serum and milk
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the AuNP/AMBI/rGO/SPCE electrode for electrochemical sensor to determine real samples was evaluated by measuring P4 in calf serum and milk. The samples were prepared as described in the experimental section and tested using the same SWV method as the standard curve test. The content of P4 in the serum sample could be achieved based on the peak current. The recoveries of P4 in calf serum and milk (Table 2 ) were 98.768% and 100.783%, respectively, and the RSD values were 1.394 and 1.940, respectively, indicating that the viscosity of calf serum and milk and the large amount of interfering substances did not affect the performance of the electrode. Consistent with the standard curve, it shows that the AuNP/AMBI /rGO/SPCE electrode has good applicability for P4 sensing in serum and milk samples.

The composite electrodes were compared with the published results in Table 3 . Although the upper limit of the linear range is smaller than the Sn-modified/GC, AuNP/MnSB/GCE electrodes, the entire linear range includes the progesterone content range in female mammals (0.96–1272 nM), and there is no risk of detection beyond the range after pretreatment (10-fold sample dilution). There is no need for pre-concentration in the electrode detection process, and no strong acid, strong alkali and high pressure are involved in the production process. These advantages collectively constitute a significant advantage of this method.

This work designed a multifunction surface composed by AuNP/AMBI/rGO on SPCE electrode. The combination of AuNPs, AMBI and rGO was confirmed by XPS, XRD and other characterization methods. The fabricated sensor possessed relevant stability and elevated reproducibility. By considering the successful application of the proposed method for P4 determination in serum and milk, this composition electrode composed sensor can be used for other analytes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin Municipality (18JCYBJC21200), the Tianjin Municipal Science and Technology Commission of China (No. 16YEXTSY00210), and Tianjin Front Tec. Ltd.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2021.115023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive electrochemical detection of progesterone using tin-nanorods modified glassy carbon electrodes: Voltammetric and computational studies
Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Das, M.V. Sangaranarayanan, A sensitive electrochemical detection of progesterone using tin-nanorods modified glassy carbon electrodes: Voltammetric and computational studies, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 256 (2018) 775–789. doi:10.1016/j.snb.2017.10.008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Manganese mediated oxidation of progesterone in alkaline medium: mechanism study and quantitative determination
Electrochim. Acta
